perturbation.original_text: eGain will provide live telephone support to Customer 24-hours-a-day, seven-days-a-week by a trained eGain customer support representative.
perturbation.changed_text: eGain will provide live telephone support to Customer during normal business hours, Monday through Friday, excluding national holidays, by a qualified customer support agent.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract specifies 24/7 support, while the modified version limits it to business hours. This change introduces ambiguity and potential conflict with laws requiring reasonable support access depending on the industry and nature of the service.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly mandating 24/7 support, the ADA requires reasonable accommodations to ensure effective communication with individuals with disabilities. Limiting support to business hours may not be considered a reasonable accommodation for some users, particularly if the service is critical or time-sensitive, potentially conflicting with the ADA's requirement for equal access.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: c accommodations include privately-owned, leased or operated facilities like hotels, restaurants, retail merchants, doctor’s offices, golf courses, private schools, day care centers, health clubs, sports stadiums, movie theaters, and so on. This title sets the minimum standards for accessibility for alterations and new construction of facilities. It also requires public accommodations to remove barriers in existing buildings where it is easy to do so without much difficulty or expense. This title directs businesses to make "reasonable modifications" to their usual ways of doing things when serving people with disabilities. It also requires that they take steps necessary to communicate effectively with customers with vision, hearing, and speech disabilities. This title is regulated and enforced by the U.S. Department of Justice. More information and events related to ADA Title III (Public Accommodations) . Title IV (Telecommunications) This title requires telephone and Internet companies to provide a nationwide system of interstate and intrastate telecommunications relay services that allows individuals with hearing and speech disabilities to communicate over the telephone. This title also requires closed captioning of federally funded public service announcements. This title is regulated by the Federal Communication Commission. More information and events related to ADA Title IV (Telecommunications) . Title V (Miscellaneous Provisions) The final title contains a variety of provisions relating to the ADA as a whole, including its relationship to other laws, state immunity, its impact on insurance providers and benefits, prohibition against retaliation and coercion, illegal use of drugs, and attorney’s fees. This title also provides a list of certain conditions that are not to be considered as disabilities. Future ADA Issues What Barriers Still Exist For People With Disabilities? perturbation.justification: The original contract guarantees 24/7 support, while the changed text limits it to business hours. The Law Explanation suggests this could conflict with the ADA if it doesn't provide reasonable accommodations for individuals with disabilities requiring access outside normal business hours, particularly for critical or time-sensitive services. The scraped snippet from ADA Title III requires 'reasonable modifications' to usual business practices for people with disabilities, and effective communication with customers who have vision, hearing, and speech disabilities. Limiting support to business hours may not meet the requirement for 'reasonable modifications,' thus creating a legal contradiction, and making the contract potentially unenforceable if it fails to accommodate these individuals.

perturbation.original_text: Payment received by eGain after the due date shall be subject to a late fee equal to one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, or, if less, the maximum amount allowed by applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: Payment received by eGain after the due date shall be subject to a late fee equal to two percent (2%) per month, regardless of any conflicting laws.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract defers to applicable laws regarding late fee limits. The modified version sets a fixed rate (2%) without regard to legal limits, creating a potential conflict with usury laws or other state regulations on late payment fees.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's usury law limits the interest rate that can be charged on loans and forbearance of money. By setting a fixed late fee of 2% per month 'regardless of any conflicting laws,' the modified text directly contradicts California's usury law if the service provided is considered a loan or forbearance of money, or if the 2% monthly rate exceeds the legally allowed maximum interest rate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies a fixed late fee of 2% per month 'regardless of any conflicting laws'. This directly contradicts the California Constitution, Article XV, which sets limits on interest rates for loans and forbearance. Snippet 1 specifies that the rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money shall be 7 percent per annum. The changed text removes the original contract's clause that defers to the applicable laws, and sets a flat rate, which violates the usury law. The modification introduces non-compliance with the law by attempting to override legally mandated interest rate limits.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California exclusive of its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Delaware, without regard to any conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies California law, while the modified version uses Delaware law. This change matters since Delaware law is much more corporate-friendly and would significantly affect the interpretations of breaches.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose a governing law, California courts may disregard that choice if it violates a fundamental public policy of California, especially if the contract significantly impacts California residents or businesses. By changing the governing law to Delaware, this modification could be challenged if it undermines protections afforded by California law to California businesses or residents, particularly relating to fair business practices or consumer protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The change from California to Delaware law contradicts the principle outlined in the Law Explanation, where California courts may disregard the chosen law (Delaware) if it violates a fundamental public policy of California, especially if the contract impacts California residents or businesses. Snippet 1, CIV 1646, states that a contract is interpreted according to the law of the place where it is to be performed or made. If the contract is performed or made in California, using Delaware law creates a contradiction because it deviates from the legally recognized place of jurisdiction, potentially undermining California's public policy and the protections afforded to its residents and businesses.
